event: Adam and Eve laboring after their expulsion from Eden
symbols: muscular laboring bodies — the physical toll of the curse, humanity condemned to toil by the sweat of the brow (Genesis 3:19)
intertwined figures — the bond between Adam and Eve persisting even after the Fall, shared labor and shared punishment
grid lines on the drawing — the artist's preparatory framework, but also suggestive of the ordered creation now subjected to the discipline of work
absence of landscape or garden — the stark emptiness surrounding the figures evokes exile from paradise, the loss of Eden's abundance
Eve's subordinate posture — the asymmetry of labor and relationship introduced by the curse (Genesis 3:16)
description: A preparatory chalk drawing on paper shows two nude figures — a muscular male and a female — in dynamic, intertwined poses suggesting physical labor. The male figure (Adam) is positioned above, his powerful torso twisting as he appears to dig or work with a tool. The female figure (Eve) is below and to the right, her body curving in a complementary pose. Red grid lines overlay portions of the composition, indicating this is a preparatory study for transfer to a larger work. The anatomical rendering is bold and confident, with elongated Mannerist proportions.
theological_description: Pontormo's study depicts the consequences of the Fall as described in Genesis 3:17-19: 'Cursed is the ground because of you; through painful toil you will eat food from it all the days of your life... By the sweat of your brow you will eat your bread until you return to the ground.' This is the first human labor — not the joyful gardening of Eden (Genesis 2:15, 'to work it and keep it') but the punitive toil that follows disobedience. The theological tradition distinguishes sharply between pre-lapsarian work (creative cooperation with God) and post-lapsarian labor (burdensome, exhausting, futile without grace). Adam and Eve working together preserves the relational structure of creation — 'It is not good for the man to be alone' (Genesis 2:18) — but now that partnership is shadowed by the distortions of the curse. Paul reads this condition cosmically: 'The creation was subjected to futility' (Romans 8:20), groaning under the burden until the redemption of all things. The muscular exertion visible in Pontormo's figures anticipates the New Testament theology of labor redeemed: Christ the carpenter (Mark 6:3) sanctifies work, and Paul commands, 'Whatever you do, work heartily, as for the Lord' (Colossians 3:23). The drawing's unfinished, preparatory quality itself becomes a metaphor: fallen humanity is a sketch awaiting completion in Christ, the final Adam (1 Corinthians 15:45) who restores what the first Adam lost.

scripture_refs.reason: God's curse upon Adam: the ground is cursed, and humanity must toil painfully for food until returning to dust
The expulsion from Eden — Adam and Eve are driven out to work the ground from which Adam was taken
The pre-Fall mandate to 'work and keep' the garden, now transformed into burdensome labor after disobedience
The curse upon Eve: pain in childbearing and the distortion of the marital relationship
Paul's cosmic reading: creation subjected to futility, groaning in labor pains until redemption — the theological framework for all post-Fall toil
The first Adam and the last Adam — fallen humanity's labor will be redeemed through Christ, the life-giving spirit
Work redeemed in Christ: 'Whatever you do, work heartily, as for the Lord' — the eschatological reversal of the curse on labor
The next generation's labor: Abel keeping flocks and Cain working the soil, continuing the toil inherited from their parents
